excerpt: A hardware signer reduces the trusted computing base for key custody from a general-purpose operating system to a small, single-purpose device with a verifying display. The display is the load-bearing security feature: the user must verify destination address and amount on the signer's screen, not on the host computer.
body:
Source excerpt:
A hardware signer reduces the trusted computing base for key custody from a general-purpose operating system to a small, single-purpose device with a verifying display. The display is the load-bearing security feature: the user must verify destination address and amount on the signer's screen, not on the host computer.

---

Cited by leaf "Hardware signers and the air-gap":
A hardware signer is a small device whose only job is to hold private keys and sign transactions you hand it. It never holds funds, never connects to the network, and ideally never speaks to anything except a microSD card or a QR code reader. The threat model it defends against is malware on your everyday computer: a laptop that's been compromised can spy on your screen, swap clipboard addresses, and watch you type, but it cannot sign a transaction without the signer's keys, and the signer's screen shows you the actual address and amount it's about to authorize [1].

"Air-gapped" means the signer has no network interface — no USB data, no Bluetooth, no Wi-Fi. Communication is via QR code or removable storage, with a partially-signed bitcoin transaction (PSBT) carried in and a signed PSBT carried out. The threat-model improvement over USB-connected signers is real but bounded: USB attacks against widely-used signers do exist in the literature, but most successful key-extraction events have been physical (chip decap, voltage glitching), not remote. The bigger practical win is workflow: you sign on a machine that has no general-purpose code execution, so phishing and malicious browser extensions cannot sneak transactions past your verification step [2].

The one structural choice that matters more than vendor selection is whether the signer's firmware and (where applicable) its hardware design are open and reproducible. Open firmware lets independent reviewers audit the signing logic, the entropy sources, and the PSBT-handling code; reproducible builds let you verify the binary on the device matches the published source [3]. This is the relevant axis to evaluate signers on. Concrete vendor advice — "buy this brand, avoid that one" — is exactly the kind of thing the project doesn't go into; for that, read independent reviews from people who do this full-time and pay attention to who has had vulnerabilities disclosed and how they handled them.

---

Cited by leaf "HD wallets and BIP32 derivation":
An HD (hierarchical deterministic) wallet derives every one of its keys from a single seed via a deterministic tree. From the seed you compute a master extended key; from that master, child keys at any path you like; from any child, more children, indefinitely [1]. The practical consequence: backing up the seed backs up the entire wallet — current keys, future keys, every account you'll ever derive from it — without needing to copy out individual private keys ever.

The derivation path you see on wallet screens — usually written `m/84'/0'/0'/0/5` or similar — encodes which branch of the tree the key lives on. The BIP44/49/84/86 conventions assign meaning to the first few levels: purpose (script type — 84' is native segwit, 86' is taproot), coin type (0' is Bitcoin), account index, change-or-receive, and address index [2]. Two wallets that agree on those conventions can both sweep the same seed and find the same coins; that's why "recover seed in any BIP39-compatible wallet" works in practice.

The `'` mark means the derivation step is hardened: the parent's private key is required to derive the child. Without hardening, the math allows a third party with the parent xpub plus any child private key to compute the parent private key — fine inside a single wallet that controls everything, catastrophic if you've shared an xpub for watch-only purposes [3]. This is why all account-level paths are hardened: it lets you safely hand out an account xpub for receive-address generation by another device without exposing the master.
